serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pasta carbonara with runny egg yolk is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked pasta tossed in an egg-yolk-and-cheese emulsion with cured pork and black pepper.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti carbonara, restaurant-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Pasta Runny Yolk Carbonara' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 168936), whole egg yolk (FDC ID 172185), bacon/guanciale (FDC ID 168319), and Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171251), weighted to a typical carbonara recipe ratio (pasta ~55%, sauce/egg ~25%, pork ~15%, cheese ~5%). The restaurant version at Flavours by Saute may include cream, which would increase fat and energy; values reflect a mid-range estimate. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta, spaghetti, white, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for carbonara pasta. GI of 47 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti, boiled (mean GI ~47, low category, ≤55). The egg-yolk-and-fat-rich sauce does not raise GI; fat and protein slow gastric emptying, which may further reduce effective GI. GL per serving calculated as: (47 × 27.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving) / 100 = 45.3, rounded to 45.0. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg/100 g; ~1330 mg per serving) from cured pork, cheese, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 55–65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (47) from pasta, partially offset by high fat and protein slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions; the low GI of pasta (approximately 47) and the fat-protein matrix of the carbonara sauce reduce postprandial glucose excursion, but the high glycemic load per full serving (~45) warrants portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.1 g/100 g; ~14 g per serving) and cholesterol (~115 mg/100 g; ~400 mg per serving) from egg yolk, cured pork, and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence; the high saturated fat and dietary cholesterol content from egg yolk and guanciale may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels, and the high sodium further elevates cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta Carbonara with Runny Egg Yolk is a restaurant-style Italian pasta dish served in Singapore, most notably at Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall. The dish is built on a base of al dente spaghetti or linguine tossed in a rich emulsion of raw or lightly cooked egg yolk, finely grated Pecorino Romano or Parmesan cheese, and rendered guanciale or bacon fat, finished with cracked black pepper. The defining feature is the runny egg yolk — either a whole yolk placed on top or incorporated into the sauce at low heat — which adds a custardy, unctuous richness and a vivid golden colour. The texture is silky and coating, with a savoury, umami-forward flavour profile from the cured pork and aged cheese. Unlike cream-based pasta sauces, an authentic carbonara achieves its creaminess entirely through the emulsification of egg and cheese with starchy pasta water. Restaurant adaptations in Singapore sometimes incorporate a small amount of cream for stability. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~245 kcal/100 g), moderate in protein (~10 g/100 g), and notable for its saturated fat (~4.1 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~115 mg/100 g) from egg yolk and pork. Sodium is elevated (~380 mg/100 g) due to cured meat and cheese. The pasta base provides a low glycemic index (~47), making postprandial glucose rise more gradual than refined-grain alternatives. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat, and requires portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes given the high glycemic load per full serving. A plant-based vegan adaptation is reportedly available at the same restaurant at no extra charge, likely substituting the egg and pork with plant-based alternatives.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for 'Pasta Runny Yolk Carbonara' was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked spaghetti, egg yolk, bacon, and Parmesan cheese weighted to a typical carbonara recipe ratio. GI is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti (GI ~47) as the closest analogous food. Confidence is medium due to recipe-level estimation rather than direct dish measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked spaghetti FDC 168936, egg yolk FDC 172185, bacon FDC 168319, Parmesan FDC 171251) weighted to a typical carbonara recipe. The restaurant version may differ if cream is added. 2. GI of 47 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti, boiled; this is the closest analogous food. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. The input description states 'Plant-based vegan option available at no extra charge' — this refers to a restaurant menu variant and does not affect the nutritional profile of the standard carbonara record. A separate vegan variant record would require independent nutritional assessment. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly measured for this specific restaurant dish; they are provided as best-estimate values. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; guanciale (pork cheek) is a traditional ingredient and the dish is not halal unless specifically prepared with halal-certified substitutes. 6. The cuisine tag 'Global' from the input is preserved; the dish is of Italian origin but served in a global-cuisine restaurant context in Singapore. 7. All numeric fields in per_100g are positive estimated values; no fields are set to -1 as component-level estimates were available for all nutrients from USDA FoodData Central.
